fig01:
%
  Grouped bar chart comparing four methods---Baseline, Method-A, Method-B, and \textbf{OursModel}---across
  five benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, MATH, GPQA).
  Error bars show the sample standard deviation over 5 independent seeds.
  Stars~({\footnotesize$\bigstar$}) mark the top-performing method per benchmark;
  \textbf{OursModel} achieves the highest accuracy on all five tasks.
  Hatching distinguishes methods for black-and-white reproduction.
  Data are synthetic but realistic (see \texttt{data.json}).%
fig02: Validation loss over 50 training steps for three methods (mean $\pm$ 95\% CI across $n{=}5$ seeds, shaded bands). OursModel converges faster and achieves the lowest final loss; the double-headed arrow marks the gap $\Delta{=}0.155$ between OursModel and the next-best Method-A at the final step.
fig03:
Chinchilla-style neural scaling law. Each point is a model run plotted by
$\log_{10}(\text{parameters})$ vs.\ $\log_{10}(\text{validation loss})$ ($n{=}80$).
The red line is an OLS fit ($y = -0.0449x + 0.621$, $R^{2}=0.877$), consistent with
the expected power-law relationship between model size and loss.
The highlighted point (\textbf{OursModel}, $x=9.449$, $y=0.210$) is the data point
nearest the target coordinates $(9.4,\,0.205)$.
fig04:
\textbf{Four-panel hero figure.}
\textbf{(A)}~Conceptual overview of the three-stage pipeline: input tokens are processed by an
Attention+Memory module, routed through a Skill Router, and decoded to an output prediction;
a residual skip connection bypasses the intermediate stages.
\textbf{(B)}~Ablation study: removing attention (\texttt{-attn}), memory (\texttt{-mem}),
the residual connection (\texttt{-residual}), or the skill router (\texttt{-skill}) each
reduces accuracy relative to the full model (64.7\%).
\textbf{(C)}~Training loss curves over 30\,k steps; our method (solid blue) converges
faster and to a lower final loss than the baseline (dashed grey).
\textbf{(D)}~Sensitivity sweep across a $5\times5$ grid of hyperparameters $S$ and $P$;
cell values indicate normalised performance scores (0--1).
